metadata.career_relevance: Every production Spring Boot application runs in multiple environments — dev, staging, production. @ConfigurationProperties keeps configuration type-safe and grouped. Spring profiles switch config sets without code changes. When you join a company's backend team, understanding profiles (the distinction between dev and prod config) and @ConfigurationProperties (the type-safe alternative to scattered @Value) is expected day one.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll bind property groups to Java records with @ConfigurationProperties, add startup validation with @Validated, use Spring profiles for environment-specific config, and test configuration in isolation.

visual_aid.description: Three panels: (1) @Value vs @ConfigurationProperties comparison: Left column - @Value scattered, no autocomplete, string keys, no validation. Right column - @ConfigurationProperties record, IDE autocomplete (dropdown shown), type-safe, @Validated. (2) Profile loading diagram: Base application.properties always loaded. Active profile (dev/prod) overlays on top — profile properties override base. Environment variable SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE shown as the switch. Table of what differs: datasource URL, JPA ddl-auto, logging level, external API URL. (3) @ConfigurationProperties record anatomy: annotation prefix, record fields, kebab-to-camel mapping ('max-retries' → maxRetries), @Validated + @NotBlank constraints, injection via constructor.
visual_aid.alt_text: @Value vs @ConfigurationProperties comparison, profile loading overlay, and record field mapping

worked_example.scenario: Refactor the payment service configuration from scattered @Value annotations to grouped @ConfigurationProperties records. Add environment profiles so dev uses a sandbox API and H2, while prod uses the real API and PostgreSQL.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Create `PaymentGatewayProps` record binding `payment.gateway.*`
2. Create `DatabaseProps` record binding `db.pool.*`
3. Add @Validated with constraints on both
4. Create dev and prod profile property files
5. Add @Profile beans for MockEmailService (dev) and RealEmailService (prod)
6. Test properties load correctly with @SpringBootTest @ActiveProfiles

step_by_step_solution.details:
```properties
# application.properties:
spring.application.name=payment-service
server.port=8080
payment.gateway.max-retries=3
payment.gateway.timeout-ms=5000

# application-dev.properties:
spring.profiles.active=dev
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:devdb
payment.gateway.url=https://sandbox.razorpay.com
payment.gateway.api-key=rzp_test_sandbox
logging.level.root=DEBUG

# application-prod.properties:
spring.datasource.url=${PROD_DB_URL}
payment.gateway.url=https://api.razorpay.com
payment.gateway.api-key=${RAZORPAY_LIVE_KEY}
logging.level.root=WARN
```

worked_example.expected_output:
# Application startup (dev profile):
[main] Active profiles: dev
[main] PaymentGatewayProps{url='https://sandbox.razorpay.com', timeoutMs=5000, maxRetries=3, ...}
[MockEmailService] Email service: MOCK (dev mode)

# Startup with missing required property:
[main] APPLICATION FAILED TO START
*** APPLICATION FAILED TO START ***
Configuration property 'payment.gateway.url' must not be blank
things_to_notice: Kebab-case in properties maps to camelCase in records: `payment.gateway.max-retries=3` binds to `maxRetries` field. Spring handles this relaxed binding automatically. Both `max-retries` and `maxRetries` in properties work.

@ConfigurationPropertiesScan on the Application class automatically discovers all @ConfigurationProperties records in the package tree — no manual registration needed. Without it, you need @EnableConfigurationProperties(MyProps.class) for each class.

@Validated on the record class triggers Bean Validation at startup. If `payment.gateway.url` is blank in application.properties, the application REFUSES to start. This fail-fast behavior is a significant advantage over @Value — a missing @Value just injects null.

@Profile({"dev", "test"}): the MockEmailService is active in both dev and test profiles. This means your @SpringBootTest (which uses 'test' profile by default) gets the mock automatically — no @MockBean needed for the email service.

Environment variable override: in production, `SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL=jdbc:postgresql://...` overrides `spring.datasource.url` in the properties file. Spring Boot maps environment variable PAYMENT_GATEWAY_API_KEY to `payment.gateway.api-key`. This is how secret values are injected in Docker/Kubernetes without putting them in files.

industry_examples.use_case: Razorpay runs dev, test, staging, and prod environments. @ConfigurationProperties binds all gateway configuration (API endpoints, timeouts, rate limits) into typed records. application-prod.properties contains no secrets — only property keys referencing env vars (${RAZORPAY_KEY}). Kubernetes injects the actual secrets as environment variables. Dev developers work with application-dev.properties pointing at sandboxes. Zero code changes between environments.

@Profile('beta') activates beans for A/B test features. @Profile('prod') activates the default path. @Profile('load-test') activates mock external services so load testing doesn't call real APIs. Spring profiles make this conditional loading zero-code — no `if (System.getenv('ENV').equals('beta'))` scattered through the codebase.

Every mature Spring Boot codebase has @ConfigurationProperties records for: database pool settings, external API credentials, cache TTLs, feature flags, CORS config, rate limits. The pattern is always the same: `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix='my.service')` on a record or class, `@Validated` with constraints, and injection via constructor. It's so common that Spring Boot generates metadata (spring-configuration-metadata.json) enabling IDE autocomplete for custom properties.

applications.interview_relevance:
@ConfigurationProperties and profiles interview questions:

- **'What is @ConfigurationProperties and when do you use it?'** → Type-safe binding of a group of properties to a Java record/class. Use when you have 3+ related properties for one component. Better than @Value: grouped, validated, IDE autocomplete.
- **'What are Spring profiles?'** → Named sets of beans and properties. Active profile determines which application-{profile}.properties is loaded and which @Profile beans are created.
- **'How do you separate dev and prod configuration?'** → application-dev.properties + application-prod.properties. Activate with spring.profiles.active or env var SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE.
- **'How do you prevent null @Value injection?'** → Use @ConfigurationProperties with @Validated @NotBlank — fails at startup if missing.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily @ConfigurationProperties workflow:

- New feature needs config: add @ConfigurationProperties record with @Validated
- Add properties to application.properties (shared defaults)
- Add overrides to application-dev.properties and application-prod.properties
- In CI/CD: set environment variables for secrets
- In @SpringBootTest: use @ActiveProfiles('test') to load test properties

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Creating the @ConfigurationProperties record but not adding @ConfigurationPropertiesScan or @EnableConfigurationProperties — properties don't bind, fields stay null.
common_gaps.remediation: Add @ConfigurationPropertiesScan to @SpringBootApplication, OR @EnableConfigurationProperties(MyProps.class). Without either, the record is not a Spring bean and cannot be injected. The @ConfigurationPropertiesScan approach is cleaner — one annotation on Application covers all records in the package.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: NullPointerException on props.url() — the record was injected but fields are null

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Creating application-prod.properties but the app still uses dev config — profile not activated.
common_gaps.remediation: application-{profile}.properties is only loaded when that profile is active. Set `spring.profiles.active=prod` in application.properties, OR pass `--spring.profiles.active=prod` as a JVM arg, OR set `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod` as an env var. In production, env var is the standard.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Production app still connects to H2 — application-prod.properties exists but isn't loaded

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Adding @NotBlank to @ConfigurationProperties fields but forgetting @Validated on the class — validation never runs.
common_gaps.remediation: Bean Validation on @ConfigurationProperties requires @Validated on the class (not just @Valid). This is different from controller validation where @Valid on the parameter triggers it. Without @Validated, @NotBlank/@Positive etc. are compiled but never evaluated.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Application starts despite blank required properties — @NotBlank had no effect

key_takeaways: @ConfigurationProperties(prefix='my.service'): binds all my.service.* properties to a record
Kebab-case maps to camelCase: my.service.max-retries → maxRetries
@Validated on the record class: fail-fast validation at startup
@NotBlank/@Min/@Max on record fields: validated at startup with @Validated
application-{profile}.properties: loaded only when that profile is active
SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod: production activation via env var
@Profile('dev') on a bean: created only when dev profile is active
@ActiveProfiles('dev') in tests: activate specific profile for a test class

self_check_questions.question: You create a `DatabasePoolProps` @ConfigurationProperties record with @NotBlank on the url field. The application starts even when the url property is missing. What's wrong?
self_check_questions.answer: @Validated is missing on the record class. Bean Validation annotations (@NotBlank, @Min etc.) on @ConfigurationProperties fields are only evaluated if the class is annotated with @Validated. Without it, the constraints are defined but never enforced. Fix: add @Validated to the record class. Also check that @ConfigurationPropertiesScan or @EnableConfigurationProperties is present — otherwise the record isn't a bean at all.

self_check_questions.question: You have application-prod.properties with real database credentials. The application starts in dev mode and still connects to the prod DB. Why?
self_check_questions.answer: spring.profiles.active is set to 'prod' somewhere — possibly in application.properties. Check: (1) application.properties for `spring.profiles.active=prod` — change to dev. (2) Environment variable SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod — override it. (3) A command-line argument --spring.profiles.active=prod. Profile-specific properties (application-prod.properties) are only loaded when that profile is active. To confirm which profile is active, check the startup log: '[main] Active profiles: prod'.

self_check_questions.question: When should you prefer @ConfigurationProperties over @Value?
self_check_questions.answer: Prefer @ConfigurationProperties when: (1) you have 3 or more related properties for one component — grouped binding is cleaner than 3+ @Value fields, (2) you want startup validation — @Validated + @NotBlank fails fast, (3) you want type safety — Duration, List, nested objects bind automatically, (4) you want IDE autocomplete — Spring generates metadata for custom @ConfigurationProperties. Use @Value for: single one-off properties, simple defaults, or quick configuration reads. Rule of thumb: if you're @Value-ing more than 2 properties from the same prefix, switch to @ConfigurationProperties.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 14 topic: **Actuator and Production Observability** — when your application is running in production, you need to know: is it healthy? What's the memory usage? How many cache hits? Is the database connection pool saturated? Spring Boot Actuator exposes these as HTTP endpoints — health checks, metrics, thread dumps, environment info. After Topic 14.5, you deploy a production-observable application.
